card.intuition: Use two stacks — one for operands, one for operators — and apply operators in precedence order: when a lower-precedence operator arrives, flush all higher-precedence operators waiting on the stack.
card.analogy: A chef juggling orders: high-priority (multiplication) orders get cooked before lower-priority (addition) orders that arrived first. The chef holds pending low-priority orders on a ticket rail until the urgent ones are done.
card.time_complexity: O(n) — each token is pushed and popped at most once
when_to_use: Evaluate infix arithmetic expressions respecting PEMDAS/BODMAS order of operations
Convert infix to postfix (Reverse Polish Notation) via Shunting-Yard algorithm
Interpret simple expression languages in interpreters or calculators
Any problem involving nested parentheses that need to be resolved inside-out
gotchas: Pop and apply the operator stack before pushing a new operator of equal or lower precedence — left-associativity means equal precedence also pops
Left parenthesis on the operator stack acts as a barrier — stop popping when you see it; discard both parentheses when a right paren arrives
Handle unary minus carefully — it's a different token from binary minus

exercise.prompt: Evaluate a simple integer expression with +, -, *, / (integer division) and no parentheses.
hints: Pop and apply waiting operators when their precedence is >= the new operator's precedence (left-associativity)
Check ops[-1] is in PREC (not a parenthesis) before comparing precedence levels
exercise.test_code:
assert evaluate('3+2*2')       == 7
assert evaluate(' 3/2 ')       == 1
assert evaluate(' 3+5 / 2 ')   == 5
assert evaluate('10-3-2')      == 5
assert evaluate('2*3+4*5')     == 26
assert evaluate('100*2+12')    == 212
print('PASS')
